original_abstract:
A revolution is reshaping the face of global education, as learning is increasingly treated as an internationally traded commodity rather than a public good. Traditionally, education was viewed as a foundational set of values and skills essential for informed citizenship; today, it is frequently repositioned as a "skill set" to be purchased by consumers or a product marketed by multinational corporations and academic institutions operating as businesses.

This transformation is most visible in the debate surrounding the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS). While global knowledge exchange has existed since the medieval era, the modern push toward commodification through the World Trade Organization poses significant risks. While globalization offers potential for economic integration and the rapid flow of information via the Internet, it also threatens to exacerbate inequalities. Wealthier nations and for-profit providers may "cream off" lucrative fields like business and IT, leaving local institutions to struggle with underfunded basic sciences and cultural studies.

Ultimately, education serves as the bedrock of civil society and national identity—elements that cannot be reduced to market transactions. Protecting intellectual independence and cultural patrimony requires a balanced approach to internationalization, ensuring that the global pursuit of knowledge does not descend into a new form of commercial neocolonialism that robs less powerful nations of their academic autonomy.
humanized_abstract:
LansCAPE: A WORLD IN TRANSFORMATION The whole world is watching as an education revolution unfolds at an unbridled pace – the revolution of education-as-a-commodity, which turns the education of young generations from a global public good into an international market of commercial value. The shift away from an educational ideal anchored in a well-defined set of civic and intellectual competences and values that ensure an active participation in democracy, towards one where education is reduced to an exchangeable commodity and thus open to market forces represents, in our view, a step back and therefore unacceptable. In its place is a very different skill-set or commodity designed to meet the demands of education's new consumers. This increasingly prevalent perspective on what learning is all about can be explained in different ways but, in the final analysis, the underlying attitude toward education is comparable to that applied to goods sold in the market and to products marketed by companies -- with universities and colleges joining their ranks as 'for-profit' businesses.

The transformation is highly visible in the context of the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS). Global knowledge exchange is a phenomenon that stretches back to the medieval period, and the pressure on that model from the World Trade Organization is a development with profound implications. On the one hand, globalisation is a model of economic integration that has the potential to facilitate global knowledge sharing through the Internet, which is a global information infrastructure. On the other hand, it can also perpetuate existing inequalities and reinforce the unsustainable trends in contemporary higher education. In particular, the current WTO Round negotiations, which in practice represent the interests of more powerful economies and private enterprise, could cause a skew in the distribution of educational disciplines. The most marketable subjects such as business studies and IT could be harvested by profit-seeking operators from other countries, while the local educational system is left to grapple with the inadequacies and limitations of the state budget in relation to the core disciplines such as physics, mathematics and the humanities.

It is primarily as the foundational element of a civil society and national identity that we think of education–as things that you cannot simply buy. Safeguarding academic freedom and cultural heritage calls for a globalization of learning that steers a middle course between the ideals of universal openness and the threat of commercial neo-colonialism. This threat implies that less developed countries are exposed to a new type of imperialism that robs them of what little degree of independence they may still possess.
